nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs, harbors, lagoons, and outer reef slopes at depths of 1–65 m

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied diet including meaty frozen foods (mysis, brine shrimp), marine flakes, and herbivore preparations. Vitamin-enriched foods have been noted to help maintain coloration.

avoidWith: fish that predate on small damsels

summary: The Orange Line Chromis is a Western Pacific damselfish generally considered hardy and beginner-friendly. Juveniles display a bright orange lateral stripe that fades to brownish coloring in adults. The species tends to do best in groups of three or more and is broadly regarded as reef-safe.
careNotes:
The Orange Line Chromis (Acanthochromis polyacanthus) is one of the few marine damselfishes that lacks a pelagic larval stage — meaning the fry skip a drifting, open-water juvenile phase and instead hatch directly into a form closely resembling miniature adults. This unusual reproductive biology makes the species relatively accessible to breed in captivity compared to most reef fish.

Juveniles display a bright blue body with a vivid orange horizontal stripe running from the gill plate to the tail; adults tend to darken considerably over time, eventually becoming brownish to near-black with a whitish tail. This color transformation can surprise keepers who acquired the fish as juveniles.

The species is considered semi-aggressive, particularly once pairs form and begin defending a nesting territory. Keeping three or more individuals together tends to distribute aggression across the group rather than focusing it on one target. Adults typically reach 10–14 cm in captivity.

The Orange Line Chromis is native to the Western Pacific — Indonesia, the Philippines, northeastern Australia, Papua New Guinea, and the Solomon Islands — and is not found in the Indian Ocean, despite occasional mislabeling by retail sources. Lifespan in captivity is generally reported in the range of 8–15 years, with some accounts noting exceptional specimens reaching approximately 18 years under ideal conditions.
breedingNotes: The Orange Line Chromis is generally regarded as one of the more accessible saltwater damselfishes to breed in captivity. Unlike most marine fish, the species skips the pelagic larval stage entirely — fry hatch at a relatively advanced stage and do not require the specialized rearing setups that open-water larvae typically demand. The recommended approach is to acquire a group of juveniles and allow them to pair naturally over time. Once a bonded pair has formed, moving them to a dedicated aquarium and providing flat rock surfaces for egg deposition tends to be productive. Both parents actively guard the eggs and fry, a behavior that is uncommon among reef fish.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (5 in vs LiveAquaria 4 in), temperament (semi-aggressive vs peaceful), diet (carnivore vs omnivore), and lifespan (up to 18 yrs vs 8-15 yrs). BlueZoo's semi-aggressive rating adopted as more conservative; diet resolved as omnivore per LiveAquaria's more detailed notes; lifespan range 8–15 from LiveAquaria adopted.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Scientific reference. Confirmed Western Pacific range (correcting LiveAquaria's 'Indian Ocean'). Max TL 16.0 cm (scientific), temperature 25.1–28.9°C (warmer than LiveAquaria's 72–78°F/22–26°C). Temperature averaged to 23–28°C. FishBase notes monogamous pairing during breeding and parental guarding of fry with no pelagic larval stage.

fish.breedingNotes: The Orange Line Chromis is generally regarded as one of the more accessible saltwater fish to breed in captivity. The species lacks a pelagic larval stage, meaning fry hatch at an advanced stage and are easier to rear than those of most reef fish. Both parents guard the eggs and fry. Acquiring a group of juveniles and allowing natural pairing is the approach most commonly recommended.
